expected_facts: Self-RAG uses learned reflection tokens to decide whether to retrieve
Active RAG (FLARE) triggers additional retrieval based on low generation confidence or token probabilities
Self-RAG is introspective/adaptive, active RAG is reactive to generation uncertainty
Both methods address the indiscriminate-retrieval problem of vanilla RAG
Tuning-based approaches like RAG-HAT train the model to avoid hallucinations
RAGTruth is a hallucination corpus used for training and evaluating detectors
Detection-based frameworks like LettuceDetect and SEReDeEP identify hallucinations at inference time
Approaches span training-time mitigation and inference-time detection
GraphRAG builds a knowledge graph from the corpus via LLM-based entity and relation extraction
Leiden or similar hierarchical community detection groups related entities
Local-to-global pattern: summarize each community, then summarize the community summaries
Designed for query-focused summarization (QFS) over an entire corpus
Legal RAG systems use specialized benchmarks like LegalBench-RAG for evaluation
Case-based reasoning (CBR-RAG) retrieves analogous prior cases as RAG context
Structural information from legal documents (citations, sections) improves case retrieval
Reliable retrieval in large legal corpora is a known challenge due to structurally similar documents
BM25-based hard negatives are a common baseline approach
Multi-stage sampling like TriSampler uses different principles for negative selection
Hard negatives matter for cross-lingual and multilingual dense retrieval
Better negatives improve embedding quality (Conan-embedding)
KG-as-prompt approaches like Knowledge Graph Prompting use graph structure in the prompt
GNN-RAG uses graph neural networks for KG retrieval
Combining KG with LLMs improves multi-hop reasoning
The roles of graphs vs LLMs in KG-QA are an active research area
RAGTruth is a hallucination corpus for RAG systems
It contains word-level hallucination annotations
Covers multiple RAG tasks (QA, summarization, data-to-text)
Used for training and evaluating hallucination detectors
Hybrid search combines sparse BM25 with dense neural retrievers
Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) is a common fusion method
Dynamic alpha tuning (DAT) adjusts the BM25/dense weight per query
Hybrid retrieval often outperforms either method alone, especially for out-of-domain queries
Specialized code embedding models like CodeXEmbed are trained for code retrieval
CodeRAG-Bench is a benchmark for evaluating retrieval-augmented code generation
Repository-level retrieval matters more than file-level for many tasks
Structural chunking of code (AST-based) outperforms naive text chunking
Medical RAG systems integrate clinical knowledge bases such as UMLS or PubMed
Domain-adapted embeddings improve biomedical retrieval over general-purpose models
RAG reduces hallucinations in clinical settings vs vanilla LLMs
Surveys document RAG applications across healthcare, biomedicine, and clinical decision support
RAGAS is an automated RAG evaluation framework
It provides reference-free metrics including faithfulness, answer relevancy, and context relevancy
Uses LLM-as-judge to score generated answers
Designed to enable RAG hyperparameter tuning without human-labeled data
Two-stage pipeline: bi-encoder retrieves candidates, cross-encoder reranks the top-K
Bi-encoders are fast but less precise; cross-encoders are precise but slow
This trade-off motivates the two-stage architecture
Distillation from cross-encoder to bi-encoder is one approach to improve efficiency
HyDE generates a hypothetical document via LLM, embeds it, retrieves on that embedding
Query2Doc generates pseudo-documents from the query and concatenates to the original query
HyDE is zero-shot and does not require relevance labels
Both leverage LLMs to bridge the query-document vocabulary gap
Multilingual dense retrievers like mDPR are used for cross-lingual RAG
Language drift and language preference are known issues in multilingual RAG
Culturally-sensitive queries require special handling beyond translation
Performance varies significantly across language pairs (not all languages are equal)
